critique.summary: OpenRocket is a free, open-source model rocket simulator that enables users to design, simulate, and optimize rocket models before physical construction and flight. It offers advanced simulation features, CAD-based design tools, motor selection from a large database, and community support through documentation and a Discord server.
mainPoints: OpenRocket provides a six-degrees-of-freedom flight simulation with over 50 variables for reliable rocket flight predictions.
Users can design rockets using CAD technology, including material properties and finish quality, and export designs as PDFs.
The software includes an AI assistant to optimize rocket designs for specific performance goals such as higher altitude or longer flight.
Real-time feedback on performance metrics like center of pressure, center of gravity, maximum altitude, velocity, and stability is available during design.
Supports multi-stage rockets, dual deployment, event triggers, and motor clustering with preset configurations.
Includes a large motor database sourced from ThrustCurve, allowing users to select appropriate and safe motors for their designs.
OpenRocket is open source, with documentation for users and developers, and a community Discord server for support and collaboration.
critique.truthfulness: The claims about OpenRocket's features are consistent with information available from the official OpenRocket website and reputable rocketry hobbyist sources. OpenRocket is widely recognized as a reliable and free open-source rocket simulation tool that offers detailed flight simulation, CAD design capabilities, and motor databases. The mention of AI optimization and real-time feedback aligns with recent software advancements. The availability of documentation and community support is also verifiable. Overall, the article accurately represents the software's capabilities without making unsupported or exaggerated claims.
considerations: The article does not mention any limitations or potential inaccuracies in simulation results compared to real-world rocket flights.
It does not discuss the learning curve or technical expertise required to effectively use the software.
There is no mention of compatibility with other rocketry software or hardware integration.
The article does not provide independent user reviews or comparisons with alternative rocket simulation tools.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers around the use and accuracy of OpenRocket software in rocketry design, with users sharing their practical experiences and comparisons to other tools. Some comments highlight the educational value and community aspects, while others touch on related software and concerns about legal implications.

comments.author: hermitcrab
comments.text:
We used OpenRocket for designing our rocket for UK youth rocketry competition UKROC[1].

It is great for getting a 'spherical cow in a vaccuum' idea of likely altitude with different motors, centre of pressure, center of mass etc. But it obviously doesn't take account of detailed aerodynamics etc and we found the maximum altitude estimates were about 15% too high. But it was still very useful.

[1] UKROC is an amazing competition for UK school kids. And there are equivalent competitions in the US, France and Japan, with an International competition for the 4 country winners. If you know any kids interested in engineering I recommend you look into it.

https://www.ukroc.com/
comments.analysis: Provides a balanced and experience-based evaluation of OpenRocket's accuracy, acknowledging its limitations while emphasizing its usefulness.

comments.author: chasd00
comments.text:
This application gets used a lot in the High Power Rocketry hobby. Most of the parts/manufactures are included as well as motor manufactures. The simulations are very good and accurate, I would sim my larger builds at the location where i was launching to get an idea of altitude and it was always pretty close ( within 5-10% i'd say ).

I use to have a website where you could upload an openrocket file and get back 2d drawings for your fins that could then be sent to my lasercutting service. The idea was design the rocket in openrocket, send me the file, and get back the wooden pieces you need cut per the design. Similar to sendcutsend but for the rocketry hobby.

Really cool seeing it show up on HN.
comments.analysis: Offers practical insights into the software's accuracy and community use, adding credibility by describing related services.

comments.author: peterus
comments.text:
Our university rocket team uses openrocket extensively for doing fast design iterations early in the design phase. We have also used Rasaero II which is meant to be more rigorous above transonic speeds. We have an Ansys CFD too but that requires significantly more time to set up. 
We still use openrocket on launch days to do pre launch sims, but we override some of the parameters based on the more rigorous simulations.
comments.analysis: Provides a nuanced view of OpenRocket's role within a broader simulation toolkit, highlighting its practicality and limitations.

comments.author: _moof
comments.text:
Software developers: I am begging you to put representative screenshots on the home page. This has become a real widespread problem.

I know you want to tell us all about the amazing (sincerely!) stuff under the hood, but to users, the interface is the product.
comments.analysis: Constructive feedback on user interface presentation, emphasizing the importance of visual communication for user engagement.

comments.author: ehnto
comments.text:
I would recommend checking out your local laws on export of software with military applications. I believe this would be illegal to release in my country (whether that's the right thing is worth discussing, but protect yourself first).

Looks like a pretty mature project though so I suppose it must be on solid ground.

It might also run the risk of breaking IMPORT laws in your respective countries, worth being sure of because that is not a realm of law you want to be messing with.
comments.analysis: Raises valid legal and ethical considerations about software export controls, though specifics depend on jurisdiction.

comments.author: perryprog
comments.text:
Tangentially related is NASA's open source GMAT[0] software which is more focused for calculating orbital transfers and the like. It's pretty fun to play around in.

[0] https://software.nasa.gov/software/GSC-18094-1
comments.analysis: Adds relevant context by mentioning related aerospace simulation software, enriching the discussion.

comments.author: EvanAnderson
comments.text: This is pretty cool. I remember having fun simulating my rockets using the BASIC programs from G. Harry Stine's "Handbook of Model Rocketry" when I was a kid. This looks like a way to recreate some of that fun.
comments.analysis: Shares nostalgic and personal perspective, adding a human element but less technical detail.

comments.author: CarVac
comments.text: I helped out with a user interface redesign of OR many years ago. It was pretty incredibly unintuitive back then, and many hobby rocketeers paid for Rocksim instead.
comments.analysis: Provides insider perspective on the software's UI challenges and competition, supporting a realistic view of its usability.

comments.author: aliljet
comments.text: I wonder how crazy the scale here can get. How far can I go? The bps.space guy is heading into space. Can the community hit the moon? Literally.
comments.analysis: Expresses aspirational curiosity about the software's potential, though speculative and not grounded in technical detail.

comments.author: swalsh
comments.text: Oh i've been looking for a project for my 11 year old... he's a very project oriented learner, which schools don't seem to do anymore.
comments.analysis: Highlights the educational value and accessibility of rocketry projects for youth, adding a personal and societal perspective.
